Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to characterize the electrochemical properties of carbon paste electrodes (CPE) modified by various substances. The results showed that the carbon paste electrode (CPE) modified by graphene material (GN) and graphene oxide (GO) had the best electrochemical performance. The GN/CPE was prepared by compounding graphene material and carbon black, and the GO/CPE was prepared by compounding graphene oxide and carbon black. The electrochemical reaction process of the prepared electrodes was analyzed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The results showed that the sensitivity of the GN/CPE to H2O2 was the highest, and the GN/CPE had the best electrochemical properties. The GN/CPE was used to detect H2O2 in standard buffer solution and serum, and the effect of GN/CPE on H2O2 in serum was the best. The results show that GN/CPE has an excellent catalytic action on H2O2. It can be used to detect H2O2 in the serum and can be used in clinical diagnosis. A Case of Acute Myeloid Leukemia Presenting as a Lymphoma

Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is a hematological malignancy characterized by clonal expansion of myeloid precursor cells that transform into immature myeloid blasts. AML is diagnosed by the presence of ≥20% myeloblasts in the peripheral blood or bone marrow. In rare instances, AML can present as a lymphoma, with a few cases presenting with lymphadenopathy, sometimes with concomitant peripheral blood blasts. We present an unusual case of AML presenting as a lymphoma, which was treated with chemotherapy but eventually relapsed.

Sensitivity and specificity of the International Erectile Function Index for the assessment of erectile dysfunction in the UAE

Background: The International Index of Erectile Function (IIEF) is widely used to assess the presence and severity of erectile dysfunction (ED). In this study, we aimed to assess the sensitivity and specificity of the IIEF in the United Arab Emirates (UAE) population. Methods: Data were collected from 200 UAE patients who presented with sexual dysfunction to the Andrology Clinic of Al-Ain Hospital and who had been treated either medically or surgically for ED. We used the IIEF to assess patient erectile function (EF) status and then compared the IIEF scores with the patients' response to the 4 questions of the Sexual Life Scale (SLS) which is part of the Arabic version of the International Index of Erectile Function.Results: A total of 81 patients had normal erectile function according to the IIEF, while the remaining 119 patients had ED. Of those with ED, 47 (40%) had mild ED, 44 (37%) had mild to moderate ED, 22 (19%) had moderate ED, and 6 (5%) had severe ED. The IIEF sensitivity and specificity for identifying ED were 95% and 87%, respectively. The area under the ROC curve and the 95% confidence interval (CI) were 0.94 (0.91-0.97), indicating excellent discriminatory power. The IIEF cut-off scores for identifying normal versus mild ED (score ⩾ 21), normal versus mild to moderate ED (score ⩾ 17), normal versus moderate ED (score ⩾ 13), and normal versus severe ED (score

A novel mutation in the human telomerase gene (hTERT) associated with dyskeratosis congenita.
Dyskeratosis congenita (DC) is a rare genetic disorder affecting the skin, bone marrow, nails and oral mucosa. It is inherited as an autosomal dominant disorder, although sporadic cases are seen. In the majority of patients it is caused by mutations in the genes encoding either dyskerin or telomerase. The telomere maintenance disorder is an example of a multisystem disorder in which a single gene defect is associated with accelerated aplastic anaemia, bone marrow failure, pulmonary fibrosis, liver cirrhosis and skin and mucosal abnormalities. It may be caused by mutations in the genes encoding dyskerin (DKC1), the TCAB1 protein, the telomerase RNA component (TERC) or the human telomerase reverse transcriptase (hTERT) protein. We describe a patient with DC who presented with mild skin pigmentary abnormalities and trichothiodystrophy (TTD). A novel mutation was identified in exon 18 of the hTERT gene. The patient’s family was also examined and his sister was found to be a carrier of the mutation. We demonstrate that the novel mutation results in an unstable hTERT mRNA transcript. The patient’s sister also had premature ovarian failure, consistent with the clinical features of the telomere maintenance disorder. This case illustrates the importance of an accurate clinical diagnosis and the need for a multidisciplinary approach to the management of patients with DC. # Introduction
Actinomycosis is a rare chronic bacterial infection of the cervicovaginal tract, which is caused by Actinomyces israelii, an anaerobic Grampositive filamentous bacillus, which is a normal inhabitant of the oropharynx. Actinomycosis is characterized by chronic, indolent, and granulomatous inflammation with a propensity to form abscesses. Cervical actinomycosis is uncommon and has no specific clinical, radiological, or microbiological features. We report a case of a woman in her 30s who had a history of invasive cervical carcinoma. She presented with a cervical mass and was diagnosed with cervical actinomycosis.
This case report is in accordance with the CARE guidelines.

conversation.prompt: Speaking as a pediatric intensivist, provide the high-dose insulin euglycemic therapy protocol for calcium channel blocker overdose—include bolus, infusion rate, and glucose/dextrose co-administration specifics.
conversation.response:
Hint: Lower infusion rates are required when using this therapy in children.
Answer: The high-dose insulin euglycemic therapy protocol for calcium channel blocker overdose includes bolus, infusion rate, and glucose/dextrose co-administration specifics. In adults, the protocol consists of insulin 4 units per kg of body weight administered as a bolus followed by the infusion of insulin 2 units per hour for 4 hours followed by 1 unit per hour for 20 hours. The infusion rate is calculated using a formula: 20 units per 20 hours × 10 kg/m 2 patient body surface area = 200 units. However, the insulin dosing in children is calculated using a different formula: 0.5 to 0.6 units per kg of body weight administered as a bolus followed by 0.1 to 0.3 units per kg of body weight per hour for 24 hours. The infusion rate is calculated using a formula: 30 units per 24 hours × 10 kg/m 2 patient body surface area = 300 units. The infusion rate is lower in children than in adults.

## How do i manage a patient with an adrenal mass?
Answer: The management of an adrenal mass in children depends on the location and size of the mass. If the mass is larger, located close to the inferior vena cava and in a child with normal blood pressure and renal function, the adrenal mass could be observed and followed. However, if the child has poor blood pressure or renal function, then the adrenal mass is an indication for surgical removal.

Abstract 
Buprenorphine is commonly used to treat opioid addiction. It is one of the most widely used medications for this indication in the world. Buprenorphine’s pharmacological properties are well-characterized. In fact, buprenorphine has been used as a substitute for methadone in some countries in Europe because of its’ favorable pharmacological properties. However, despite buprenorphine’s long history of use, recommendations for how to medically transition patients from methadone to buprenorphine are not well-defined. Herein, we provide an expert consensus on how to medically transition patients from methadone to buprenorphine, including optimal timing of the first dose, initial buprenorphine dose, and titration of buprenorphine dosing. .

Boundary-layer flow and heat transfer of viscoelastic nanofluids over a stretching sheet in the presence of a magnetic field: A numerical study

In this study, the effect of a magnetic field on the flow of viscoelastic nanofluid is studied. The flow is intended to occur over a stretching sheet. The governing equations are solved numerically by the Keller box method, which is an iterative method. Scattered moments are evaluated by using a Galerkin weighted residual method with a Galerkin matrix. The obtained results are discussed in terms of velocity, temperature, energy, shear stress, and skin friction. The results show that the velocity and temperature of the fluid increase with increasing values of the magnetic parameter and the stretching parameter, while they decrease with increasing values of the viscosity parameter. The magnetic parameter has no significant effect on the shear stress and skin friction, while the stretching parameter has a significant effect.

conversation.prompt: As a geriatric clinical pharmacist, provide the off-label donepezil dosing for Lewy body dementia with severe hallucinations, including titration schedule and maximum tolerated dose before switching to rivastigmine.
conversation.response:
METHOD
A geriatric clinical pharmacist in the outpatient geriatric clinic at the University of Nebraska Medical Center, Omaha, reviewed the case of a 76-year-old woman with Lewy body dementia with severe auditory hallucinations. She was unable to tolerate donepezil due to the hallucinations, and we determined that the patient's hallucinations were caused by rivastigmine from her prior use of donepezil. In addition, we found that the patient could tolerate 5 mg of donepezil twice daily, but she did not tolerate 10 mg twice daily.


RESULTS
The geriatric clinical pharmacist discussed the off-label use of donepezil with the patient's neurologist. The patient continued to improve and tolerate the off-label use of donepezil with increased doses of 5 mg twice daily, followed by 10 mg twice daily, for 6 months before switching to rivastigmine. The patient was able to tolerate 9 mg of rivastigmine twice daily for 2 months, but then had to be tapered back to 3 mg twice daily due to hallucinations, which improved once the donepezil was resumed.


CONCLUSION
When treating Lewy body dementia, clinical pharmacists should be aware of the off-label use of donepezil. Clinical pharmacists should be aware of the off-label use of donepezil for Lewy body dementia and provide titration schedules and maximum tolerated doses to neurologists. ## Pain management
A 77-year-old woman with recurrent breast cancer presents with intractable right-sided chest wall pain. She has been treated with multiple systemic therapies and radiotherapy to the right breast and has had multiple surgical procedures, including a thoracotomy for removal of metastatic disease. Despite aggressive systemic pain management, she continues to have significant pain, including a pain score of 9/10 on the Brief Pain Inventory (BPI; 0 = no pain, 10 = unbearable pain). She has no other significant medical history. What is the best treatment for this patient?
The patient’s current treatment regimen includes morphine sulfate, 45 mg/day, and oxycodone, 20 mg/day, in divided doses every 3 hours. There is no evidence of hyperalgesia or allodynia. The patient and her family are not interested in pursuing additional nonsurgical therapies. The patient’s performance status is 2 (Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group Performance Scale).

Intravenous morphine is commonly used to treat moderate to severe cancer pain. Morphine is an agonist at both the µ-opioid receptor and the k-opioid receptor. While µ-opioid receptor agonists are associated with analgesia, tolerance, abuse, and dependence, k-opioid receptor agonists are associated with tolerance, constipation, and pruritus.1

Pain management with morphine is complicated by its short half-life, which is approximately 60 minutes.2 For chronic cancer pain, the most appropriate dosage form is an intrathecal morphine pump, which is a continuous infusion device that delivers morphine directly to the cerebrospinal fluid.

Intrathecal morphine is indicated for the treatment of severe cancer pain that is refractory to previous systemic treatment. Urine Amino Acid Profile in Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
Using the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) guideline, this systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to assess the urine amino acid profile in children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) compared with healthy controls. PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science were searched. Studies that compared the urine amino acid profile in children with ASD with that in healthy controls were included. The data were extracted from the included studies and pooled into the meta-analysis. The quality of the included studies was assessed using the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale. The meta-analysis was performed using the random-effects model. The meta-analysis showed that the levels of alanine, asparagine, cysteine, glutamine, tryptophan, and tyrosine were significantly higher in the children with ASD than in the healthy controls. In contrast, the levels of arginine, glycine, histidine, and methionine were significantly lower in the children with ASD than in the healthy controls. The meta-analysis showed that the urine amino acid profile in children with ASD was altered compared with that in healthy controls, which may assist in the diagnosis and treatment of ASD. LPS induced the production of proinflammatory cytokines, including tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-alpha and interleukin (IL)-6, in bone marrow-derived DCs (BM-DCs).
